welcome.title: Atomic Physics Lecture
welcome.body: Interactive, offline simulations for teaching atomic physics. Use the controls to change each experiment and watch the physics respond in real time.
welcome.hint: Select a topic from the left to begin.

about.text: An offline lecture aid for atomic physics. Interactive simulations cover the structure of the atom, quantum phenomena, X-rays, and the foundations of quantum mechanics. All simulations use real physical constants. No internet connection or external service is required.

sec1.title: Sec.1  The Element of the Atom — Electron

ch2.title: Ch.2  Quantum Nature of the Atom

ch3.title: Ch.3  The Basics of Quantum Mechanics

cathode_ray.title: Electric Charge & the Discovery of the Electron
cathode_ray.desc: Thomson's cathode-ray tube. Electrons fired from the cathode pass between charged plates and a magnetic field. The beam bends toward the positive plate — proof that cathode rays carry negative charge.

millikan.title: Determining the Mass & Charge of the Electron
millikan.desc: Millikan's oil-drop experiment. A charged oil drop is balanced between two plates so that the electric force cancels gravity. The measured charge is always a whole-number multiple of the elementary charge e.

mass_spec.title: Mass Analysis of Matter (Mass Spectrometer)
mass_spec.desc: Ions are accelerated by a voltage and bent by a magnetic field. Heavier isotopes follow a wider arc, so they land at different detector positions. The peak heights reproduce the natural isotopic abundances.

cross_section.title: Effective Cross-section & Mean Free Path
cross_section.desc: A beam is fired into a slab of target atoms. Each particle travels straight until it strikes a target's effective cross-section. Averaged over many particles, the distance before a collision is the mean free path λ = 1 / (n σ). Compare the theoretical λ with the measured value, and watch the beam attenuate.

planetary.title: The Nuclear (Planetary) Model of the Atom
planetary.desc: Rutherford's model: nearly all the mass and all the positive charge sit in a tiny central nucleus, with electrons orbiting far outside — like planets around the sun. Turn on classical radiation to see why this model is unstable: an orbiting electron would radiate energy and spiral inward, a problem later solved by Bohr.
planetary.scale_note: Not to scale: a real nucleus is ~10⁴–10⁵ times smaller than the atom.

cloud_chamber.title: Alpha-Particle Tracks in a Cloud Chamber
cloud_chamber.desc: The Geiger–Marsden gold-foil experiment, seen through the tracks the alpha particles leave — as in a Wilson cloud (mist) chamber. Almost all pass nearly straight through, showing the atom is mostly empty space, while a rare few deflect sharply or bounce backward, revealing a tiny, dense, positive nucleus. Turn on 'Keep tracks' to let the pattern build up.

rutherford.title: The Rutherford Scattering Formula
rutherford.desc: The quantitative law behind the experiment. In SINGLE mode, set the impact parameter b and watch one alpha's hyperbolic path; the measured deflection angle is compared live with the formula tan(θ/2) = kZ/(E·b). In BEAM mode, many alphas rain onto the nucleus and the detector histogram builds up the Rutherford distribution, ∝ 1/sin⁴(θ/2), overlaid as a smooth curve.

spectral_density.title: Spectral Energy Density
spectral_density.desc: Thermal radiation is described by a spectral energy density u(λ,T): the energy held in each small band of wavelength. This is the measured blackbody spectrum at temperature T. Move the cursor to pick a band dλ and read the fraction of the total energy it contains — the meaning of 'u dλ = energy in [λ, λ+dλ]'.

bb_features.title: Features of Blackbody Radiation
bb_features.desc: The classical regularities. Kirchhoff's law: a cavity with a small hole is the ideal blackbody (a good absorber is a good emitter). Wien's displacement law: the peak wavelength shifts as λ_max = b/T. Stefan–Boltzmann law: the total radiated power per area grows as σT⁴. Ghost curves show cooler and hotter temperatures so the peak shift is clear.

rayleigh_jeans.title: The Rayleigh–Jeans Law
rayleigh_jeans.desc: The classical prediction u = 8πkT/λ⁴ agrees with experiment at long wavelengths but diverges as λ → 0, implying infinite total energy — the 'ultraviolet catastrophe'. It is shown here (dashed red) against the actual Planck spectrum (blue) on the same axes.

planck.title: Quantum Radiation — Planck's Law
planck.desc: Planck assumed energy is exchanged only in quanta E = hν. That one idea gives u(λ,T) = 8πhc/λ⁵ · 1/(e^{hc/λkT} − 1), which fits experiment and removes the catastrophe. Drag the 'h scale' toward zero: as h → 0 the quantum curve collapses onto the classical Rayleigh–Jeans curve and the catastrophe returns.

pe_apparatus.title: Experimental Principle of the Photoelectric Effect
pe_apparatus.desc: Monochromatic light strikes a metal cathode in a vacuum tube; ejected electrons cross to the anode and register as a photocurrent. A variable voltage accelerates or retards them. The reverse voltage that just stops the current — the stopping potential V_s — measures the maximum electron energy: e·V_s = KE_max.
pe_apparatus.below: hν < W : no electrons are emitted

wave_theory.title: Wave Theory of Light and the Photoelectric Effect
wave_theory.desc: Classical wave theory predicts that electron energy should grow with light intensity, that any frequency should work if bright enough, and that dim light should show a delay while energy is absorbed. Every one of these disagrees with experiment, where the energy depends only on frequency, a threshold frequency exists, and emission is instant.

quantum_theory.title: Quantum Theory of Light and the Photoelectric Effect
quantum_theory.desc: Einstein: light comes in photons of energy E = hν, and one photon gives all its energy to one electron, so KE_max = hν − W. Below the threshold ν₀ = W/h no electrons escape, however bright the light. Plotting KE_max against frequency gives a straight line of slope h — exactly as measured.

applications.title: Applications of the Photoelectric Effect
applications.desc: Two devices built on the photoelectric effect. A photomultiplier tube turns a single photon into a large pulse by cascading electrons down a chain of dynodes (gain ≈ δ^N). A solar cell uses photons to create electron-hole pairs in a p-n junction, driving a current through an external load — light converted straight into electric power.
applications.pmt_note: one photon → one electron → an avalanche down the dynodes
applications.solar_note: photons create electron–hole pairs that drive the load current

prism_spectroscope.title: Features of the Atomic Spectrum — Prism Spectroscope
prism_spectroscope.desc: A prism disperses light by wavelength. A hot solid or incandescent filament produces a *continuous* spectrum — all colours are present. A gas-discharge lamp emits only discrete *lines* at wavelengths fixed by the element. Switch sources and compare: continuous blackbody radiation versus the fingerprint line spectra of hydrogen, helium, and sodium.

frank_hertz.title: Frank–Hertz Experiment
frank_hertz.desc: Electrons are accelerated from a cathode through a gas-filled tube to a collector. When the electron energy reaches the first excitation energy E₀ of the gas atoms, inelastic collisions drain kinetic energy and the collector current drops. Repeating dips at E₀, 2E₀, 3E₀, … prove that atomic energy levels are quantised.

hydrogen_spectrum.title: Hydrogen Atomic Spectrum
hydrogen_spectrum.desc: Hydrogen emits (or absorbs) light only at discrete wavelengths given by the Rydberg formula 1/λ = R_H(1/n₁² − 1/n₂²). The Balmer series (n₁ = 2) lies in the visible; Lyman (n₁ = 1) is ultraviolet; Paschen (n₁ = 3) is infrared. Each line corresponds to a photon released when the electron drops between Bohr levels.

planetary_flaws.title: Disadvantages of the Planetary Model
planetary_flaws.desc: Rutherford's nuclear atom places electrons in classical orbits, but classical physics predicts an atom that cannot exist: electrons should radiate and spiral inward, emit continuous spectra instead of sharp lines, and have no reason to choose one orbit radius over another. Step through each failure — the motivation for Bohr's quantised orbits.
planetary_flaws.rad_note: A charge in circular motion accelerates continuously. Larmor's formula says it must radiate electromagnetic energy and lose orbital energy — spiralling into the nucleus in about 10⁻¹¹ s.
planetary_flaws.spec_note: Classical orbits of any radius should produce all wavelengths. Hydrogen instead emits only the Balmer lines (and others) at fixed wavelengths — evidence of quantised energy levels.
planetary_flaws.stab_note: Nothing in classical mechanics picks a unique orbit radius. Electrons could orbit at any distance with any energy — yet atoms have definite sizes and discrete spectra.

bohr_model.title: Bohr's Atomic Model
bohr_model.desc: Bohr (1913) proposed that electrons occupy only certain stationary orbits with quantised angular momentum L = nħ. On these orbits the electron does not radiate. Light is emitted only when the electron jumps between orbits. This gives discrete radii r_n = n²a₀ and energies E_n = −13.6 eV/n².
bohr_model.post1: ① Electrons move in allowed circular orbits with L = nħ.
bohr_model.post2: ② On a stationary orbit the electron does not radiate.
bohr_model.post3: ③ Photons are emitted or absorbed only in discrete jumps between orbits.
bohr_model.formulas: r_n = n² a₀     ·     E_n = −13.6 eV / n²     ·     ΔE = hν

bohr_hydrogen.title: Hydrogen Atom — Electron Orbit & Spectrum Series
bohr_hydrogen.desc: In the hydrogen atom the electron moves on quantised Bohr orbits (left). Each orbit n has radius r_n = n²a₀ and energy E_n = −13.6/n² eV. When the electron drops from orbit n₂ to n₁ it emits a photon of wavelength λ from the Rydberg formula — one line of the Balmer, Lyman, or Paschen series (below). Change the orbit and series to see how electron motion and the line spectrum are linked.
bohr_hydrogen.formula: 1/λ = R_H (1/n₁² − 1/n₂²)   ·   each line = one orbit-to-orbit transition

xray_discovery.title: Discovery of X-Rays
xray_discovery.desc: In 1895 Wilhelm Röntgen noticed that a cathode-ray tube made a nearby fluorescent screen glow, even through black paper. The invisible rays passed through flesh but were blocked by bone. Electrons accelerated by a high voltage strike a metal target and produce X-rays by bremsstrahlung and inner-shell transitions.
xray_discovery.note: λ_min = hc / (eV)  —  higher voltage → shorter wavelength → more penetrating

xray_wave.title: Wave Property of X-Rays
xray_wave.desc: X-rays exhibit wave behaviour: they interfere and diffract. Bragg's law  nλ = 2d sin θ  predicts bright reflection from crystal planes when the path difference equals an integer number of wavelengths — the foundation of X-ray crystallography.
xray_wave.bragg_val:
n = {n}
λ = {lam} pm
d = {d} pm
θ = {theta}°
2d sin θ = {n}λ

xray_particle.title: Particle Property of X-Rays
xray_particle.desc: X-rays also behave as photons of energy E = hν = hc/λ. A single keV photon can eject a photoelectron from an atom if its energy exceeds the binding energy of that shell — the photoelectric effect extended to hard X-rays.
xray_particle.equation: E_photon ({E} keV) = W ({W} keV) + KE ({ke} keV)

compton.title: Compton Effect
compton.desc: Arthur Compton (1923) showed that X-rays scattered by electrons emerge with a longer wavelength. The shift Δλ = (h/mc)(1 − cos θ) depends only on the scattering angle θ, proving that photons carry momentum as well as energy.
compton.formula_val:
Δλ = λ_C (1 − cos θ)
     = {shift} pm  at θ = {theta}°
compton.note: Δλ is independent of λ — key evidence for photon momentum

light_duality.title: Wave–Particle Duality of Light
light_duality.desc: Light exhibits both wave and particle properties. In interference and diffraction it spreads and produces fringes like a wave; in the photoelectric effect and Compton scattering it arrives as discrete photons of energy E = hν. Neither picture alone is sufficient — this complementarity is a cornerstone of quantum mechanics.
light_duality.wave_note: Bright and dark fringes arise from superposition — light behaves as a wave.
light_duality.particle_note: Each photon ejects at most one electron. Energy depends on frequency, not intensity.
light_duality.complement: Both descriptions are needed — wave–particle duality (complementarity)

de_broglie.title: de Broglie Waves
de_broglie.desc: de Broglie (1924): every moving particle has an associated matter wave λ = h/p = h/(mv). If light can be a particle, matter can be a wave. The wavelength is tiny for macroscopic objects but comparable to atomic spacings for electrons.
de_broglie.formula_note:
p = mv

Electron at a few ×10⁶ m/s:
λ ~ 0.1–1 nm
→ atomic dimensions
de_broglie.note: The wave is not a physical vibration — it describes quantum probability

davisson_germer.title: Davisson–Germer Experiment
davisson_germer.desc: Davisson and Germer (1927) scattered electrons from a nickel crystal and found intensity peaks at specific angles — electron diffraction. The peak angles match the Bragg condition using the de Broglie wavelength λ = h/√(2meV), confirming that electrons are matter waves.
davisson_germer.note: Bragg: nλ = d sin θ   ·   Ni spacing d ≈ {d} pm   ·   peak near 50° at V ≈ 54 V
